ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic crab food product class; no specific BBQ banana leaf crab entry exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers cooked crab as the nearest parent; no banana-leaf BBQ preparation code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 is the generic crab food concept; no BBQ or banana-leaf preparation subtype exists.

serving.description: 1 whole crab (500–600 g live weight, approximately 300–350 g edible yield after shell)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one whole BBQ banana leaf crab of 500–600 g live weight, yielding approximately 300–350 g of edible crab meat and shell-on portions after grilling.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 15139); adapted for BBQ banana leaf preparation with marinade contribution estimated from analogous grilled crab recipes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy cooked crab values used as the primary nutrient base. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect BBQ marinade (soy sauce, salt, spices) typical of Singapore-style banana leaf crab preparations. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo generic cooked crab entries. Serving size based on 500–600 g live-weight crab with approximately 55% edible yield post-cooking.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate for cooked shellfish/crab
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for BBQ banana leaf crab. Cooked shellfish and crab are very low-carbohydrate foods (approximately 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g). GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 320 g serving = (5 × 1.8 × 3.2) / 100 ≈ 0.3, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.guidance: A single serving of BBQ banana leaf crab may contribute approximately 1,344 mg sodium (420 mg per 100 g × 320 g serving), representing over 55% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-marinade preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated glycemic load of 0.3 per serving, BBQ banana leaf crab has minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from marinade and moderate dietary cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While crab is a lean, high-protein seafood with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, the BBQ marinade significantly elevates sodium content; individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake and note that a 320 g serving provides approximately 250 mg dietary cholesterol, approaching the upper threshold recommended by some clinical guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: BBQ Banana Leaf Crab (烧烤香蕉叶螃蟹) is a Singapore-Chinese seafood dish in which a whole live mud crab or Sri Lankan crab of 500–600 g is marinated in a spiced BBQ sauce — typically incorporating garlic, lemongrass, chilli, soy sauce, and aromatic spices — then wrapped tightly in banana leaves and grilled or barbecued over charcoal or a gas flame. The banana leaf acts as a natural steaming and flavour-infusion wrapper, imparting a subtle grassy, smoky aroma to the crab flesh while retaining moisture. The result is tender, juicy crab meat with a caramelised, slightly charred exterior and a complex savoury-spicy flavour profile. This dish is a signature offering at Singapore seafood restaurants and zi char establishments, often served as a centrepiece dish for group dining. Nutritionally, crab is an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 18 g per 100 g), vitamin B12, selenium, zinc, and phosphorus. The fat content is low (approximately 2.6 g per 100 g) with a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated ratio. Carbohydrate content is minimal (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g), making this dish suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly meal plans when consumed without high-sugar sauces. The primary clinical concern is sodium, as the BBQ marinade can contribute over 1,300 mg sodium per serving, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is generally considered halal when prepared without alcohol-based marinades.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked crab data, which is an approved secondary source. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for BBQ banana leaf crab was found; values are consistent with HPB generic cooked crab entries. Sodium has been adjusted upward to reflect marinade contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology for low-carbohydrate seafood. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level entry in a primary approved source was identified.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked crab (NDB 15139) adapted for BBQ banana leaf preparation. Sodium (420 mg/100 g) is estimated to reflect typical BBQ marinade contribution; raw cooked crab sodium is approximately 295 mg/100 g per USDA. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy crab entry and is not available from the adapted source. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for cooked crab — naturally zero for this seafood product. 4. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g; confirmed zero for cooked crab meat per USDA SR Legacy — naturally zero as crab contains no plant-derived fibre. 5. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous low-carbohydrate seafood in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this dish exists. 6. Serving size of 320 g represents the approximate edible yield from a 500–600 g live-weight crab after shell removal and cooking loss. 7. Halal status assumes preparation without alcohol-based marinades, as is standard at halal-certified Singapore seafood restaurants. 8. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology entry for BBQ banana leaf crab exists in any of the three catalogues.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean dish (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Adapted USDA SR Legacy cooked crab nutrient values with sodium adjustment for BBQ marinade contribution.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg) and all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, dietary_fibre_g) with justification phrases in notes.
Estimated GI at 5 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous seafood methodology; documented estimation basis.
Selected closest_parent ontology codes for FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT with gap notes; used only allowed match_type values.
Verified aliases_by_language entries are verbatim consistent with flat aliases list.
Set gi_category to 'low' consistent with GI = 5 (≤ 55 threshold).
Included Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name field and machine key type2_diabetes.
